questions.question: Explain the role of the depth buffer (Z-buffer) in hidden surface removal, and how it handles depth values after perspective division.
keyPoints: Z-buffer stores depth (z) of each pixel to determine visibility
Depth values are normalized to [0,1] after perspective division
Compares depth of incoming polygon to stored depth, updating if closer
Operates on a per-pixel basis, ensuring accurate hidden surface removal

questions.question: Compare the Z-buffer algorithm and the Painter's algorithm in terms of time complexity, handling intersecting polygons, and memory requirements.
keyPoints: Z-buffer has O(n) time complexity per pixel (n: polygons), while Painter's is O(n log n) for sorting
Painter's algorithm fails with intersecting polygons; Z-buffer handles them correctly
Z-buffer requires additional memory for depth and frame buffers (per pixel), Painter's uses sorted list
Z-buffer is hardware-accelerated in modern GPUs; Painter's is software-based and simpler

questions.question: Provide a detailed step-by-step explanation of the Z-buffer algorithm, including pseudo-code, time and space complexity analysis, and discuss how it handles depth values with perspective projection. Also, mention its advantages and limitations.
keyPoints: Pseudo-code: initialize depth buffer to max, frame buffer to background; for each polygon, compute depth for each pixel; if depth < stored depth, update both buffers
Time complexity: O(n × m) where n: polygons, m: pixels per polygon; space: O(w × h) for buffers (w,h: image dimensions)
Perspective division normalizes depth to [0,1] using projection matrix; Z-buffer stores linear depth, requiring correction for perspective accuracy
Advantages: handles arbitrary polygons, simple to implement, hardware-supported. Limitations: memory intensive, aliasing artifacts, no object-space sorting

questions.question: Explain the List Priority algorithms (e.g., Newell and Newell-Sancha) for hidden surface removal. How do they compare with the Z-buffer in terms of accuracy and performance? Include an example scenario where List Priority fails and Z-buffer succeeds.
keyPoints: List Priority sorts polygons by depth (e.g., farthest first) using bounding boxes or plane equations, then renders in order (Painter's algorithm)
Newell-Newell: sorts by minimum z of polygon vertices; Sancha improves withbox overlap tests
Comparison: List Priority is O(n log n) but fails with intersecting/cyclic polygons; Z-buffer is O(n) per pixel but more memory intensive
Example: Two intersecting triangles with cyclic depth (e.g., T-shape overlap) where List Priority cannot resolve order, but Z-buffer resolves per-pixel
